[{"_id": "694f5530284b10b11dc0a869", "filename": "A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt", "text": "UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their lived informatics experiences. We develop a 
model characterizing tracker processes of deciding to track 
and selecting a tool, elaborate on tool usage during collection, 
integration,  and  reflection  as  components  of  tracking  and 
acting,  and  discuss  the  lapsing  and  potential  resuming  of 
tracking.  We  use  our  model  to  surface  underexplored 
challenges  in  lived  informatics,  thus  identifying  future 
directions for personal informatics design and research.  

Author Keywords 
Lived Informatics; Personal Informatics; Self-Tracking; 
Lapsing; Physical Activity; Finances; Location. 

ACM Classification Keywords 
H.5.m. Information interfaces and presentation (e.g., HCI). 

INTRODUCTION 
Personal informatics, or collecting and reflecting on personal 
information,  has  become  increasingly  prevalent.  Personal 
informatics can serve a goal-driven purpose, such as tracking 
weight loss, increasing physical activity, having a record of 
places  visited,  or  tracking  student  loan  payments.  Beyond 
these goal-driven motivations, self-tracking tools are now a 
part  of  everyday  life,  or  “lived  informatics”,  with  people 
tracking out of curiosity or a fascination with numbers [34]. 
The pervasiveness of self-tracking tools has led researchers 
to  study  people’s  use  of  commercial  tools  for  tracking 
location  [24,30],  finances  [20],  food  [11],  weight  [19,25], 
and  physical  activity  [16,34]  and  to  develop  research 
prototypes to expand our understanding of usage [3,8,12,40].

The first and most commonly used model for understanding 
how  people  use  personal  informatics  tools  is  Li  et  al.’s 
five-stage model [21]. The model characterizes how people 
transition  between  preparation,  collection,  integration, 
reflection, and action, describing the iterative nature of these 
stages  and  the  barriers  that  prevent  transitions.  The  model 
emphasizes an end goal of knowledge supporting behavior 
change.  The  model  has  since  been  expanded  and  clarified, 
dividing  and  combining  stages.  For  goal-oriented  trackers, 
reflection  can  be  divided  into  maintenance  and  discovery 
[22].  For  some  trackers,  engagement  with  and  gaining  an 
understanding  of  personal  data  may  be  the  goal  in  itself, 
causing the action stage to be foregone [41]. Reflection often 
occurs  during  collection  rather  than  being  a  separate  stage 
[6], which leads to an alternative three-stage iterative model 
of configuration, data capture, and feedback [7]. 

However,  research  on  everyday  self-tracking  behavior 
identifies several uses of personal informatics not described 
by  current  models.  For  example,  the  five-stage  model 
describes  self-trackers  switching  tools  to  better  fit  their 
information  needs  [21].  People  also  switch  tools  because 
their  devices  break  [16,34],  they  change  phones  [34],  or 
because 
[34]. 
Self-trackers also fail to sustain the habit or get frustrated and 
give up on tools [11]. Articulating a model that encompasses 
these behaviors can offer better guidance to designers.

To develop this model, we studied self-tracking of a variety 
of behaviors to understand how lived informatics manifests 
in the habits of self-trackers. Specifically, we surveyed 105, 
99,  and  83  past  and  present  trackers  of  physical  activity, 
finances,  and  location  on  their  habits  and  conducted  22 
interviews. We report on how people decide to start tracking 
and pick a tool, how they use that tool, how and why people 
switch  between  tools,  and  why  people  stop  tracking.  We 
develop  an  expanded  model  that  captures  the  practices  of 
self-trackers  motivated  to  track  to  change  behaviors, 
instrumenting an activity, and curiosity. 

Specifically, we find: 
•  In  addition  to  being  motivated  to  track  in  support  of 
behavior change goals, people start tracking to instrument 
an activity or out of pure curiosity. 

•  People’s  motivations  for  tracking  guide  the  selection  of 
tracking  tools.  Behavior-change  trackers  do  substantial 
background  research  and  optimize  a  tool  for  their  goal, 
instrumental  trackers  try  to  maximize  their  benefit  from 
the  tools,  and  curiosity-driven  trackers  often  do  not 
actively consider tool selection. 

731

 
 
 
 
 •  Usage of tools varies by domain and reason for tracking, 
primarily  because  people’s  motivations  are  different  in 
each domain. Behavior change trackers collect and review 
data  frequently  and  are  bothered  by  inaccurate  logs, 
instrumental trackers track when the benefits outweigh the 
effort, and curiosity-driven trackers are motivated by how 
interesting they find the data they are tracking. 

•  People frequently switch tools, and in doing so learn about 

what and how they are interested in tracking.  

•  People  lapse  in  their  tool  use  in  four  different  ways: 
track,  difficulty  managing  upkeep, 

forgetting 
intentionally skipping entries, and suspending tracking. 

to 

These  findings  inform  a  revised  model  of  how  people  use 
self-tracking  tools,  independent  of  original  motivation, 
supplementing  the  stage-based  model  for  behavior-change 
tracking. This informs a set of open design problems: 
•  Designing to resume tracking after a lapse, including how 
to use historical data collected before, or even during, the 
lapse.  For  example,  should  a  food  journal  present 
someone’s  previous  eating  habits  when  they  decide  to 
resume tracking to aid in weight loss? If so, how? 

•  Designing to avoid inertia around a long-used tool that no 
longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring

args.code:
import json
import re

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-17131210383825528866']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

extracted_papers = []
for doc in paper_docs:
    filename = doc['filename']
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    year_match = re.search(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text)
    year = int(year_match.group(0)) if year_match else None

    domain_match = re.search(r'domain: ([a-zA-Z0-9, ]+)', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    domain = domain_match.group(1).lower() if domain_match else ""

    if year == 2016 and 'physical activity' in domain:
        extracted_papers.append({'title': title, 'year': year, 'domain': domain})

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(extracted_papers))

import json
import re

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-17131210383825528866']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

extracted_papers_info = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    filename = doc['filename']
    title = filename.replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the text (case-insensitive)
    if re.search(r'physical activity', text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Try to extract the year from the first 500 characters, as publication year is usually early
        year = None
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text[:500])
        if year_match:
            year = int(year_match.group(0))

        if year == 2016:
            extracted_papers_info.append({'title': title, 'year': year})

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(extracted_papers_info))

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Check if 'physical activity' is in the text (case-insensitive)
    is_physical_activity_domain = bool(re.search(r'physical activity', text, re.IGNORECASE))

    if is_physical_activity_domain:
        # Try to extract the year, typically found early in the document
        year = None
        # Search for 2016, potentially preceded by 'CHI '' or similar conference format
        # and limit the search to the first 1000 characters to make it more likely to be the publication year
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            year = int(year_match.group(0))

        if year == 2016:
            filtered_papers.append({'title': title})

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(filtered_papers))

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_in_physical_activity_domain = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Check if 'physical activity' is in the text (case-insensitive) for domain
    if re.search(r'physical activity', text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Attempt to extract year 2016. Look for '2016' or a common conference year format like "'16" at the beginning of the paper.
        # The publication year is typically found early in the document. Let's check the first 2000 characters.
        year_match = re.search(r'(2016|\'16)\b', text[:2000], re.IGNORECASE)
        if year_match:
            # If '16 is found, assume it refers to 2016 in the context of academic papers.
            papers_in_physical_activity_domain.append({'title': title, 'year': 2016})

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(papers_in_physical_activity_domain))

longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring 

732

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

Figure 1. Li et al.’s five-stage model of personal informatics,

args.code:
import pandas as pd
import json
import re

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-3401056580358679613']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

df_papers = pd.DataFrame(paper_docs)

def extract_info(text):
    year_match = re.search(r'\b(19|20)\d{2}\b', text[:1000])
    year = int(year_match.group(0)) if year_match else None
    domain_match = re.search(r'domain: ([a-zA-Z0-9, ]+)', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    domain = domain_match.group(1).lower() if domain_match else ""
    return pd.Series({'year': year, 'domain': domain})

df_papers[['year', 'domain']] = df_papers['text'].apply(extract_info)

df_papers['title'] = df_papers['filename'].str.replace('.txt', '')

filtered_papers = df_papers[(df_papers['year'] == 2016) & (df_papers['domain'].str.contains('physical activity'))]

result = filtered_papers[['title', 'year', 'domain']].to_json(orient='records')
print("__RESULT__:")
print(result)

args.code:
import json
import re

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-3401056580358679613']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_2016_physical_activity = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Check for 'physical activity' anywhere in the text (case-insensitive) for domain
    if re.search(r'physical activity', text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for year 2016. Common patterns for publication year are at the beginning.
        # Let's extend the search to the first 2000 characters to be more flexible.
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', text[:2000], re.IGNORECASE)
        if year_match:
            papers_2016_physical_activity.append({'title': title})

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(papers_2016_physical_activity))

import json
import re

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

for doc in paper_docs:

    text = doc['text']

    # Check for 'physical activity' anywhere in the text (case-insensitive) for domain
    if re.search(r'physical activity', text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for year 2016. Common patterns for publication year are at the beginning.

        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', text[:2000], re.IGNORECASE)
        if year_match:
            papers_2016_physical_activity.append({'title': title})

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(papers_2016_physical_activity))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-3401056580358679613']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # Added a non-capturing group for ' (single quote) to avoid syntax error in case it's the culprit.
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-3401056580358679613']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # Added a non-capturing group for ' (single quote) to avoid syntax error in case it's the culprit.
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-3401056580358679613']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Robustly search for publication year 2016. Looking for common patterns like '2016' or ''16'.
        # Focus on the first few lines as publication year is typically near the beginning.
        # Clean up any potential non-printable characters before regex search.
        cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x00-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x7f]', '', text[:2000]) # Remove common non-printable ASCII characters
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', cleaned_text)
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

[{"_id": "694f5530284b10b11dc0a869", "filename": "A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt", "text": "UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their lived informatics experiences. We develop a 
model characterizing tracker processes of deciding to track 
and selecting a tool, elaborate on tool usage during collection, 
integration,  and  reflection  as  components  of  tracking  and 
acting,  and  discuss  the  lapsing  and  potential  resuming  of 
tracking.  We  use  our  model  to  surface  underexplored 
challenges  in  lived  informatics,  thus  identifying  future 
directions for personal informatics design and research.  

Author Keywords 
Lived Informatics; Personal Informatics; Self-Tracking; 
Lapsing; Physical Activity; Finances; Location. 

ACM Classification Keywords 
H.5.m. Information interfaces and presentation (e.g., HCI). 

INTRODUCTION 
Personal informatics, or collecting and reflecting on personal 
information,  has  become  increasingly  prevalent.  Personal 
informatics can serve a goal-driven purpose, such as tracking 
weight loss, increasing physical activity, having a record of 
places  visited,  or  tracking  student  loan  payments.  Beyond 
these goal-driven motivations, self-tracking tools are now a 
part  of  everyday  life,  or  “lived  informatics”,  with  people 
tracking out of curiosity or a fascination with numbers [34]. 
The pervasiveness of self-tracking tools has led researchers 
to  study  people’s  use  of  commercial  tools  for  tracking 
location  [24,30],  finances  [20],  food  [11],  weight  [19,25], 
and  physical  activity  [16,34]  and  to  develop  research 
prototypes to expand our understanding of usage [3,8,12,40]. 

Permission  to  make  digital  or  hard  copies  of  all  or  part  of  this  work  for
personal or classroom use is granted without fee provided that copies are 
not made or distributed for profit or commercial advantage and that copies
bear  this  notice  and  the  full  citation  on  the  first  page.  Copyrights  for
components  of  this  work  owned  by  others  than  ACM  must  be  honored.
Abstracting  with  credit  is  permitted.  To  copy  otherwise,  or  republish,  to
post on servers or to redistribute to lists, requires prior specific permission
and/or a fee. Request permissions from permissions@acm.org. 
UbiComp '15, September 7-11, 2015, Osaka, Japan. 
Copyright 2015 © ACM 978-1-4503-3574-4/15/09...$15.00. 
http://dx.doi.org/10.1145/2750858.2804250 

The first and most commonly used model for understanding 
how  people  use  personal  informatics  tools  is  Li  et  al.’s 
five-stage model [21]. The model characterizes how people 
transition  between  preparation,  collection,  integration, 
reflection, and action, describing the iterative nature of these 
stages  and  the  barriers  that  prevent  transitions.  The  model 
emphasizes an end goal of knowledge supporting behavior 
change.  The  model  has  since  been  expanded  and  clarified, 
dividing  and  combining  stages.  For  goal-oriented  trackers, 
reflection  can  be  divided  into  maintenance  and  discovery 
[22].  For  some  trackers,  engagement  with  and  gaining  an 
understanding  of  personal  data  may  be  the  goal  in  itself, 
causing the action stage to be foregone [41]. Reflection often 
occurs  during  collection  rather  than  being  a  separate  stage 
[6], which leads to an alternative three-stage iterative model 
of configuration, data capture, and feedback [7]. 

However,  research  on  everyday  self-tracking  behavior 
identifies several uses of personal informatics not described 
by  current  models.  For  example,  the  five-stage  model 
describes  self-trackers  switching  tools  to  better  fit  their 
information  needs  [21].  People  also  switch  tools  because 
their  devices  break  [16,34],  they  change  phones  [34],  or 
because 
[34]. 
Self-trackers also fail to sustain the habit or get frustrated and 
give up on tools [11]. Articulating a model that encompasses 
these behaviors can offer better guidance to designers.  

receive  a  new 

recommendation 

they 

To develop this model, we studied self-tracking of a variety 
of behaviors to understand how lived informatics manifests 
in the habits of self-trackers. Specifically, we surveyed 105, 
99,  and  83  past  and  present  trackers  of  physical  activity, 
finances,  and  location  on  their  habits  and  conducted  22 
interviews. We report on how people decide to start tracking 
and pick a tool, how they use that tool, how and why people 
switch  between  tools,  and  why  people  stop  tracking.  We 
develop  an  expanded  model  that  captures  the  practices  of 
self-trackers  motivated  to  track  to  change  behaviors, 
instrumenting an activity, and curiosity. 

Specifically, we find: 
•  In  addition  to  being  motivated  to  track  in  support  of 
behavior change goals, people start tracking to instrument 
an activity or out of pure curiosity. 

•  People’s  motivations  for  tracking  guide  the  selection  of 
tracking  tools.  Behavior-change  trackers  do  substantial 
background  research  and  optimize  a  tool  for  their  goal, 
instrumental  trackers  try  to  maximize  their  benefit  from 
the  tools,  and  curiosity-driven  trackers  often  do  not 
actively consider tool selection. 

731

 
 
 
 
 •  Usage of tools varies by domain and reason for tracking, 
primarily  because  people’s  motivations  are  different  in 
each domain. Behavior change trackers collect and review 
data  frequently  and  are  bothered  by  inaccurate  logs, 
instrumental trackers track when the benefits outweigh the 
effort, and curiosity-driven trackers are motivated by how 
interesting they find the data they are tracking. 

•  People frequently switch tools, and in doing so learn about 

what and how they are interested in tracking.  

•  People  lapse  in  their  tool  use  in  four  different  ways: 
track,  difficulty  managing  upkeep, 

forgetting 
intentionally skipping entries, and suspending tracking. 

to 

These  findings  inform  a  revised  model  of  how  people  use 
self-tracking  tools,  independent  of  original  motivation, 
supplementing  the  stage-based  model  for  behavior-change 
tracking. This informs a set of open design problems: 
•  Designing to resume tracking after a lapse, including how 
to use historical data collected before, or even during, the 
lapse.  For  example,  should  a  food  journal  present 
someone’s  previous  eating  habits  when  they  decide  to 
resume tracking to aid in weight loss? If so, how? 

•  Designing to avoid inertia around a long-used tool that no 
longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring 

732

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

Figure 1. Li et al.’s five-stage model of personal informatics,
reprinted  with  permission  [21].  Pri

import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Encode to utf-8 and decode, ignoring errors, to remove problematic characters
    cleaned_text = text.encode('utf-8', 'ignore').decode('utf-8')

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data from the database for all titles in 2016, then filter later
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Encode to utf-8 and decode, ignoring errors, to remove problematic characters
    cleaned_text = text.encode('utf-8', 'ignore').decode('utf-8')

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data from the database for all titles in 2016, then filter later
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

    # Replace null bytes and other non-printable ASCII characters that might cause errors

    # Replacing with a space to avoid joining words unexpectedly.
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x00-\x1F\x7F]', ' ', text)

    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):

        # The regex '\\b(2016|\\'16)\\b' handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year.
        year_match = re.search(r'\\b(2016|\\\'16)\\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Replace null bytes and other non-printable ASCII characters that might cause errors
    # Using a more comprehensive range for control characters and replacing them with a space or empty string.
    # Replacing with a space to avoid joining words unexpectedly.
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x00-\x1F\x7F]', ' ', text)

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # The regex '\\b(2016|\\'16)\\b' handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year.
        year_match = re.search(r'\\b(2016|\\\'16)\\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data from the database for all titles in 2016, then filter later
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Attempt to decode with utf-8, ignoring errors to remove problematic bytes
    try:
        cleaned_text = text.encode('utf-8', 'ignore').decode('utf-8')
    except UnicodeDecodeError:
        # Fallback if the above fails for some reason, though it should be robust
        cleaned_text = text.encode('ascii', 'ignore').decode('ascii')

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # The regex '\\b(2016|\\'16)\\b' handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year.
        year_match = re.search(r'\\b(2016|\'16)\\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Attempt to decode with utf-8, ignoring errors to remove problematic bytes
    try:
        cleaned_text = text.encode('utf-8', 'ignore').decode('utf-8')
    except UnicodeDecodeError:
        # Fallback if the above fails for some reason, though it should be robust
        cleaned_text = text.encode('ascii', 'ignore').decode('ascii')

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # The regex '\\b(2016|\\'16)\\b' handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year.
        year_match = re.search(r'\\b(2016|\'16)\\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Replace problematic characters (null bytes, non-printable ASCII) with a space
    # This is a more robust way to handle potentially corrupted text data.
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x00-\x1F\x7F]', ' ', text)

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # Using a raw string for regex pattern to avoid issues with backslashes.
        year_match = re.search(r'\\b(2016|\'16)\\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Aggressively clean the text to remove null bytes and other control characters
    # before any regex operations to prevent SyntaxError.
    cleaned_text = text.replace('\x00', '')  # Remove null bytes first
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x01-\x1F\x7F]', '', cleaned_text) # Remove other control characters

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # The regex handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year.
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Robust text cleaning: encode to bytes and then decode, ignoring or replacing problematic characters
    # This is often effective for null bytes and other unprintable characters
    cleaned_text = text.encode('utf-8', 'ignore').decode('utf-8')
    # Additionally, remove any remaining control characters that might still cause issues after initial decode
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x00-\x1F\x7F]', '', cleaned_text)


    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # Using a raw string for the regex pattern to correctly handle backslashes and apostrophes.
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Extract a small segment of the text (e.g., first 2000 characters) for targeted search
    # The publication year and domain are usually mentioned in the abstract or introduction.
    text_snippet = text[:2000]

    # Aggressively clean the text snippet to remove all non-ASCII characters
    # This is a robust way to handle any lingering encoding issues or control characters.
    cleaned_text_snippet = text_snippet.encode('ascii', 'ignore').decode('ascii')

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text snippet (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    is_physical_activity_domain = bool(re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text_snippet, re.IGNORECASE))

    # Search for publication year 2016 within the same cleaned text snippet.
    # The regex handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year.
    is_year_2016 = bool(re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', cleaned_text_snippet))

    if is_physical_activity_domain and is_year_2016:
        papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Convert to string and replace null characters explicitly before processing
    # Using str(text) to ensure it's a string, then replace null bytes.
    cleaned_text = str(text).replace('\x00', '')
    # Further clean to remove other non-printable ASCII characters that might cause issues
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x01-\x08\x0B\x0C\x0E-\x1F\x7F]', '', cleaned_text)

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # The regex handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year.
        # Using raw string for regex pattern and careful with escaping single quotes.
        year_match = re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Explicitly remove null characters and other problematic control characters
    # This replacement should happen before any regex parsing.
    cleaned_text = text.replace('\x00', '') # Remove null bytes
    # Use regex to remove other non-printable ASCII control characters (0x01-0x1F and 0x7F)
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x01-\x1F\x7F]', '', cleaned_text)

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # The regex handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year. Using raw string.
        year_match = re.search(r'\\b(2016|\'16)\\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-10605555887100927036']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Extract a small, reliable snippet from the beginning of the text for targeted search
    # The publication year and domain are usually mentioned in the abstract or introduction.
    text_snippet = text[:500] # Limiting to first 500 characters for initial check

    # Explicitly remove null characters from the snippet
    cleaned_text_snippet = text_snippet.replace('\x00', '')
    # Also remove other common non-printable ASCII characters that might cause issues for regex
    cleaned_text_snippet = re.sub(r'[\x01-\x1F\x7F]', '', cleaned_text_snippet)

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text snippet (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    is_physical_activity_domain = bool(re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text_snippet, re.IGNORECASE))

    # Search for publication year 2016 within the same cleaned text snippet.
    # The regex handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year. Using raw string.
    is_year_2016 = bool(re.search(r'\b(2016|\'16)\b', cleaned_text_snippet))

    if is_physical_activity_domain and is_year_2016:
        papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

[{"_id": "694f5530284b10b11dc0a869", "filename": "A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt", "text": "UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their lived informatics experiences. We develop a 
model characterizing tracker processes of deciding to track 
and selecting a tool, elaborate on tool usage during collection, 
integration,  and  reflection  as  components  of  tracking  and 
acting,  and  discuss  the  lapsing  and  potential  resuming  of 
tracking.  We  use  our  model  to  surface  underexplored 
challenges  in  lived  informatics,  thus  identifying  future 
directions for personal informatics design and research.  

Author Keywords 
Lived Informatics; Personal Informatics; Self-Tracking; 
Lapsing; Physical Activity; Finances; Location. 

ACM Classification Keywords 
H.5.m. Information interfaces and presentation (e.g., HCI). 

INTRODUCTION 
Personal informatics, or collecting and reflecting on personal 
information,  has  become  increasingly  prevalent.  Personal 
informatics can serve a goal-driven purpose, such as tracking 
weight loss, increasing physical activity, having a record of 
places  visited,  or  tracking  student  loan  payments.  Beyond 
these goal-driven motivations, self-tracking tools are now a 
part  of  everyday  life,  or  “lived  informatics”,  with  people 
tracking out of curiosity or a fascination with numbers [34]. 
The pervasiveness of self-tracking tools has led researchers 
to  study  people’s  use  of  commercial  tools  for  tracking 
location  [24,30],  finances  [20],  food  [11],  weight  [19,25], 
and  physical  activity  [16,34]  and  to  develop  research 
prototypes to expand our understanding of usage [3,8,12,40]. 

Permission  to  make  digital  or  hard  copies  of  all  or  part  of  this  work  for
personal or classroom use is granted without fee provided that copies are 
not made or distributed for profit or commercial advantage and that copies
bear  this  notice  and  the  full  citation  on  the  first  page.  Copyrights  for
components  of  this  work  owned  by  others  than  ACM  must  be  honored.
Abstracting  with  credit  is  permitted.  To  copy  otherwise,  or  republish,  to
post on servers or to redistribute to lists, requires prior specific permission
and/or a fee. Request permissions from permissions@acm.org. 
UbiComp '15, September 7-11, 2015, Osaka, Japan. 
Copyright 2015 © ACM 978-1-4503-3574-4/15/09...$15.00. 
http://dx.doi.org/10.1145/2750858.2804250 

The first and most commonly used model for understanding 
how  people  use  personal  informatics  tools  is  Li  et  al.’s 
five-stage model [21]. The model characterizes how people 
transition  between  preparation,  collection,  integration, 
reflection, and action, describing the iterative nature of these 
stages  and  the  barriers  that  prevent  transitions.  The  model 
emphasizes an end goal of knowledge supporting behavior 
change.  The  model  has  since  been  expanded  and  clarified, 
dividing  and  combining  stages.  For  goal-oriented  trackers, 
reflection  can  be  divided  into  maintenance  and  discovery 
[22].  For  some  trackers,  engagement  with  and  gaining  an 
understanding  of  personal  data  may  be  the  goal  in  itself, 
causing the action stage to be foregone [41]. Reflection often 
occurs  during  collection  rather  than  being  a  separate  stage 
[6], which leads to an alternative three-stage iterative model 
of configuration, data capture, and feedback [7]. 

However,  research  on  everyday  self-tracking  behavior 
identifies several uses of personal informatics not described 
by  current  models.  For  example,  the  five-stage  model 
describes  self-trackers  switching  tools  to  better  fit  their 
information  needs  [21].  People  also  switch  tools  because 
their  devices  break  [16,34],  they  change  phones  [34],  or 
because 
[34]. 
Self-trackers also fail to sustain the habit or get frustrated and 
give up on tools [11]. Articulating a model that encompasses 
these behaviors can offer better guidance to designers.  

receive  a  new 

recommendation 

they 

To develop this model, we studied self-tracking of a variety 
of behaviors to understand how lived informatics manifests 
in the habits of self-trackers. Specifically, we surveyed 105, 
99,  and  83  past  and  present  trackers  of  physical  activity, 
finances,  and  location  on  their  habits  and  conducted  22 
interviews. We report on how people decide to start tracking 
and pick a tool, how they use that tool, how and why people 
switch  between  tools,  and  why  people  stop  tracking.  We 
develop  an  expanded  model  that  captures  the  practices  of 
self-trackers  motivated  to  track  to  change  behaviors, 
instrumenting an activity, and curiosity. 

Specifically, we find: 
•  In  addition  to  being  motivated  to  track  in  support  of 
behavior change goals, people start tracking to instrument 
an activity or out of pure curiosity. 

•  People’s  motivations  for  tracking  guide  the  selection  of 
tracking  tools.  Behavior-change  trackers  do  substantial 
background  research  and  optimize  a  tool  for  their  goal, 
instrumental  trackers  try  to  maximize  their  benefit  from 
the  tools,  and  curiosity-driven  trackers  often  do  not 
actively consider tool selection. 

731

 
 
 
 
 •  Usage of tools varies by domain and reason for tracking, 
primarily  because  people’s  motivations  are  different  in 
each domain. Behavior change trackers collect and review 
data  frequently  and  are  bothered  by  inaccurate  logs, 
instrumental trackers track when the benefits outweigh the 
effort, and curiosity-driven trackers are motivated by how 
interesting they find the data they are tracking. 

•  People frequently switch tools, and in doing so learn about 

what and how they are interested in tracking.  

•  People  lapse  in  their  tool  use  in  four  different  ways: 
track,  difficulty  managing  upkeep, 

forgetting 
intentionally skipping entries, and suspending tracking. 

to 

These  findings  inform  a  revised  model  of  how  people  use 
self-tracking  tools,  independent  of  original  motivation, 
supplementing  the  stage-based  model  for  behavior-change 
tracking. This informs a set of open design problems: 
•  Designing to resume tracking after a lapse, including how 
to use historical data collected before, or even during, the 
lapse.  For  example,  should  a  food  journal  present 
someone’s  previous  eating  habits  when  they  decide  to 
resume tracking to aid in weight loss? If so, how? 

•  Designing to avoid inertia around a long-used tool that no 
longer  supports  a  self-tracker’s  goal.  For  example,  how 
can a financial tracking tool integrate historical data and 
provide an intuitive interface to a person who has managed 
their finances with a custom spreadsheet for years? 

•  Designing to support people’s various goals and transitions 
between  them.  How can the design of a location-tracking 
application  like  Foursquare  support  someone  who,  upon 
receiving a badge for frequent check-ins, becomes curious 
or  even  concerned  about  how  much  they  spend  at 
restaurants?  Can  a  location  diary  like  Moves  show 
someone who tries the application out of curiosity how it 
can help them achieve their fitness goals? 

BACKGROUND 
This  section  reviews  prior  work  on  models  of  personal 
informatics  use,  research  examining  how  people  use  tools, 
and how that use changes over time. 

Models of Personal Informatics 
Li et al. develop a stage-based model of personal informatics 
systems  and  describe  barriers  people  face  in  each  stage 
(Figure 1) [21]. The model has five stages: 1) preparation, or 
starting to collect data and thinking about what information 
to  record  and  how  to  collect  it;  2)  collection,  or  gathering 
data;  3)  integration,  or  preparing  data  to  reflect  upon; 
4) reflection, or examining and exploring data; and 5) action, 
or  taking  one’s  newfound  understanding  of  themselves  to 
inform changes. Motivated by the Transtheoretical Model of 
Behavior  Change  (TTM)  [32],  Li  et  al.  later  identify  two 
phases  of  reflection,  discovery  and  maintenance,  and  note 
people ask different types of questions in each phase [22]. 

From  studying  practices  of  the  Quantified  Self  movement, 
Choe  et  al.  learn  that  reflection  often  occurs  when  data  is 
captured  [6]  and  propose  a  new  model  for  self-monitoring

import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-14807658129369541779']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Robust text cleaning: encode to bytes and then decode, ignoring errors.
    # This is often the most effective way to handle problematic characters.
    cleaned_text = text.encode('utf-8', errors='ignore').decode('utf-8')

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    if re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE):
        # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
        # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
        # The regex handles both '2016' and shortcut ''16' for the year.
        year_match = re.search(r'\\b(2016|\'16)\\b', cleaned_text[:1000])
        if year_match:
            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

        year_match = re.search(r'\b2016\b', cleaned_text[:1000])

            papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-14807658129369541779']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Robust text cleaning: encode to utf-8, ignore errors, then decode.
    # This handles most non-printable and invalid characters.
    cleaned_text = text.encode('utf-8', errors='ignore').decode('utf-8')

    # Further remove any remaining ASCII control characters (0x00-0x1F and 0x7F)
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x00-\x1F\x7F]', '', cleaned_text)

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    is_physical_activity_domain = bool(re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE))

    # Search for publication year '2016' specifically within the first 1000 characters.
    # This is to ensure we are capturing the publication year and not a citation year.
    is_year_2016 = bool(re.search(r'\b2016\b', cleaned_text[:1000]))

    if is_physical_activity_domain and is_year_2016:
        papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Debugging print to see if any papers were filtered
# print(f"__DEBUG__: Number of papers matching criteria: {len(papers_of_interest)}")

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging, only if papers_of_interest is not empty
if papers_of_interest:
    df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)
else:
    # If no papers match, create an empty DataFrame with expected columns to avoid KeyError later
    df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(columns=['title'])

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-14807658129369541779']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Robust text cleaning: replace null bytes and other control characters
    # This is done before any regex operations to prevent SyntaxErrors.
    cleaned_text = text.replace('\x00', '') # Remove null bytes
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x01-\x1F\x7F]', '', cleaned_text) # Remove other non-printable ASCII control characters

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    is_physical_activity_domain = bool(re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE))

    # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
    # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
    is_year_2016 = bool(re.search(r'\b2016\b', cleaned_text[:1000]))

    if is_physical_activity_domain and is_year_2016:
        papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))

args.code:
import json
import re
import pandas as pd

paper_docs_path = locals()['var_function-call-14807658129369541779']

with open(paper_docs_path, 'r') as f:
    paper_docs = json.load(f)

papers_of_interest = []

for doc in paper_docs:
    title = doc['filename'].replace('.txt', '')
    text = doc['text']

    # Robust text cleaning: replace null bytes and other control characters
    # This is done before any regex operations to prevent SyntaxErrors.
    # Using .replace for null bytes first as re.sub might have issues with it in some environments.
    cleaned_text = text.replace('\x00', '')
    # Remove other non-printable ASCII control characters (0x01-0x1F and 0x7F)
    # Use raw string for the regex pattern.
    cleaned_text = re.sub(r'[\x01-\x1F\x7F]', '', cleaned_text)

    # Check for 'physical activity' in the cleaned text (case-insensitive) for domain matching
    is_physical_activity_domain = bool(re.search(r'physical activity', cleaned_text, re.IGNORECASE))

    # Search for publication year 2016 within the first 1000 characters of the cleaned text.
    # This helps in identifying the actual publication year and not a citation year.
    # Using a raw string for the regex pattern.
    is_year_2016 = bool(re.search(r'\\b2016\\b', cleaned_text[:1000]))

    if is_physical_activity_domain and is_year_2016:
        papers_of_interest.append({'title': title})

# Convert to DataFrame for easier merging
df_filtered_papers = pd.DataFrame(papers_of_interest)

# Get citation data that was previously retrieved for citation_year 2016
citation_data = locals()['var_function-call-11875698536211243064']
df_citations = pd.DataFrame(citation_data)

# Convert citation_count to numeric for summation
df_citations['citation_count'] = pd.to_numeric(df_citations['citation_count'])

# Merge the two dataframes on title
merged_df = pd.merge(df_filtered_papers, df_citations, on='title', how='inner')

# Group by title and sum citation counts
final_result = merged_df.groupby('title')['citation_count'].sum().reset_index()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(final_result.to_json(orient='records'))
